coredata.dc:title: Robust carbon quantum dots supported cobalt oxide composite particles prepared by hydrothermal process for green hydrogen generation via sodium borohydride hydrolysis

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanostructures with a particle size of 10 nm, exhibit important properties such as easy surface functionalization, low cost, chemical inertness, water solubility and low toxicity. In this study, an effective catalyst was synthesized using cobalt oxide (Co3O4) metal nanoparticles dispersed on CQD support material obtained from sucrose by hydrothermal method (Co3O4-SCQD-HT). The resulting catalyst system was used for effective hydrogen (H2) production from sodium borohydride (NaBH4) hydrolysis. Hydrogen generation rate (HGR) values of 7041, 18,426 and 27,555 mlmin−1gcat −1 were obtained with Co3O4-SCQD catalysts synthesized during the hydrothermal process with water, ethanol and methanol solvents, respectively. The HGR values of the catalysts obtained in ethanol and methanol medium provided an improvement of approximately 2.5 and 4 times, respectively, compared to the catalyst prepared in water medium. The activation energy for NaBH4 hydrolysis by Co3O4-SCQD-HT was 29.92 kJmol−1. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), nitrogen adsorption/desorption, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were used for the characterization study. The average particle size for the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite is approximately 3.27 nm. The presence of carbon (C), oxygen(O), nitrogen (N) and cobalt (Co) atoms was confirmed by XPS and EDS analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanostructures with a particle size of 10 nm, exhibit important properties such as easy surface functionalization, low cost, chemical inertness, water solubility and low toxicity. In this study, an effective catalyst was synthesized using cobalt oxide (Co3O4) metal nanoparticles dispersed on CQD support material obtained from sucrose by hydrothermal method (Co3O4-SCQD-HT). The resulting catalyst system was used for effective hydrogen (H2) production from sodium borohydride (NaBH4) hydrolysis. Hydrogen generation rate (HGR) values of 7041, 18,426 and 27,555 mlmin−1gcat −1 were obtained with Co3O4-SCQD catalysts synthesized during the hydrothermal process with water, ethanol and methanol solvents, respectively. The HGR values of the catalysts obtained in ethanol and methanol medium provided an improvement of approximately 2.5 and 4 times, respectively, compared to the catalyst prepared in water medium. The activation energy for NaBH4 hydrolysis by Co3O4-SCQD-HT was 29.92 kJmol−1. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), nitrogen adsorption/desorption, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were used for the characterization study. The average particle size for the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite is approximately 3.27 nm. The presence of carbon (C), oxygen(O), nitrogen (N) and cobalt (Co) atoms was confirmed by XPS and EDS analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen energy has attracted attention as an ideal type of energy due to its zero carbon dioxide emissions and high combustion heat [1]. The chemical storage material for hydrogen, NaBH4, is considered promising due to its 10.8 wt% hydrogen storage capacity and controllable hydrolysis reaction [2–4]. Despite the interest of many researchers in applying NaBH4 as a chemical hydrogen source in fuel cell technology, the extremely slow spontaneous hydrolysis of NaBH4 necessitates the use of a catalyst [5,6]. Therefore, there is a need for high-performance and inexpensive catalysts for the use of NaBH4 as a hydrogen source in fuel cell technology [7–10]. It is important to develop a practical H2 production by designing a durable and high-performance catalyst. The good catalytic performance using different metals for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 may be achieved [11]. Catalysts developed with noble metals such as palladium and platinum have an important place in NaBH4 hydrolysis [12,13]. However, the high cost of such catalysts is a major handicap. Consequently, there is great interest in non-noble metal catalysts on suitable substrate supports due to their lower cost compared to noble metals [14,15]. Currently, metal catalysts, especially Co-based ones, are attracting attention due to their relatively low price and high activity [16,17]. However, these catalytic systems have low performance due to their tendency to form large aggregates [18]. In addition, such catalysts have limited selectivity and are prone to deactivation or degradation over time under harsh reaction conditions [19]. As a result, approaches such as the use of catalytic supports and the use of transition metals as promoters have come to the forefront to increase the performance in such catalytic systems. Among the different support materials used for this purpose, carbon-based ones are the best with their advantageous properties such as high porosity and large specific surface area [20]. These carbon materials as a support material can effectively prevent the agglomeration of nanoparticles during the preparation and reaction process of metal-containing catalytic systems [21]. Thus, the catalytic activity is improved [22,23]. In addition, it is known that the preparation of catalysts in solvent systems with different physical and chemical properties such as viscosity and polarity affect their catalytic performance. However, this approach is quite limited for catalysts used for H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis [24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanostructures with particle size of 10 nm and below, exhibit important properties such as easy surface functionalization, low cost, chemical inertness, water solubility and low toxicity [25–29]. Active functional groups such as C-OH, NH and C-O-C on CQDs can facilitate the design of new nanocomposites by interacting with the active functional groups of other molecules [30]. All biomass carbon sources that are natural, cheap, non-toxic and have comprehensive properties can be used as carbon sources in the preparation of CQDs [31–33]. Hydrothermal method, chemical oxidation method, microwave method, hydrothermal carbonization method and pyrolysis carbonization method can be used to prepare CQDs from these carbon sources [34–39]. In this study, sucrose was preferred as the carbon source for CQD due to its easy availability and low cost. In the literature, carbon nanodots derived from sugar by hydrothermal carbonization have been reported for use in different application areas [40,41].

In this study, an effective catalyst was synthesized using Co3O4 metal nanoparticles on CQD support material obtained from sucrose by hydrothermal method. The resulting catalyst system was used for effective H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis. Optimization of hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal activation times, Co metal loading and different solvent environment parameters were carried out for the synthesis of Co3O4 metal nanoparticles on CQD from sucrose. NaBH4 concentration, NaOH concentration and temperature parameters were optimized for H2 production experiments. TEM, EDS, nitrogen adsorption/desorption, XRD, FTIR, ICP-OES and XPS analyses were used for the characterization study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Co doped carbon quantum dot synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CQD from sucrose (Sigma-Aldrich) by hydrothermal carbonization method, some sucrose and pure water were placed in a Teflon reactor in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. Filtration was performed on the obtained SCQD with a 0.45-µm filter to remove impurities. After this process, the SCQD content was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 60 min. Catalysts obtained by co-precipitation, impregnation and hydrothermal methods were named Co-SCQD, Co-sucrose and Co3O4-SCQD-HT, respectively. Co (0) was used for comparison purposes. After loading Co2+ metal ions onto sucrose CQD molecules under hydrothermal conditions, Co@SCQD-HT catalyst was obtained by reduction process with NaBH4. This process was carried out by hydrothermal loading of 2, 3, 5 and 7 mmol CoCl2x6H2O metal salt corresponding to 0.01 mol fixed sucrose amount in a 100 mL autoclave at a temperature range of 120–180 °C for 2–16 h at different loading times. As a result of hydrothermal loadings, reduction process was carried out under 10 M NaBH4 at 5000 rpm for 45 min under room conditions. In the reduction process where NaBH4 was used as a reductant (precipitant) at room conditions, the pH of the 100 mL metal-support material solution was initially 8.2, while this value was measured as 9.4–9.6 as a result of the reduction process. The impregnation process was carried out by dissolving CoCl2x6H2O salt in 25 mL of pure water at room conditions (5 mmol CoCl2x6H2O metal salt corresponding to 0.01 mol sucrose) and dropping it onto the support material at 1000 rpm for 3–4 h. The reduction process duration was accepted as the pause of bubble emergence. As a result of the reduction process, the filtered catalyst was dried in nitrogen atmosphere at 70 °C for 8 h. As a result of the loading processes carried out based on NaBH4 hydrolysis, the best catalytic activity was achieved in the loading of 5 mmol CoCl2·6H2O metal salt corresponding to 0.01 mol sucrose amount at 150 °C for 4 h.

Ultraviolet (UV) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra for SCQD were measured using a Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescent spectrophotometer. The XRD pattern (PANalytical Empyrean) for Co3O4-CQD-HT was measured in the range of 2θ:10°-80° with the radiation source Cu Κα (λ = 1.0546 Å). Information about the nanostructures of the composites was collected by TEM (Hitachi High Tech HT7700). Information about the elemental components, ratios and binding energies of the composites was collected by XPS (Specs-Flex spectrometer)). The elemental contents and ratios of the composite catalysts were investigated by EDS analysis (ZEISS Sigma 300). The surface functional groups of the composites were examined with FT-IR (Nicolet 520) spectrum in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. BET surface area and BJH pore size distributions were determined by nitrogen adsorption/desorption analysis (Quantachrome Nova 2200e) of the prepared composite. The Co metal ratio in the composite by ICP-OES was measured (Agilent 7800 model). Optical properties of SCQD with a UV–Vis/fluorescence spectrophotometer were investigated (Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescent). The highest UV emission value for the Co3O4-SCQD-HT solution was captured at 283 nm. From this emission value, it was determined that the excited Co3O4-SCQD-HT solution gave a maximum PL absorption at 318 nm.

To improve the H2 production rate from the hydrolysis of NaBH4 with the Co3O4 supported SCQD catalyst (30 mg), a certain amount of NaBH4 and a certain ratio of NaOH were added into the jacketed tube in a water bath. The reaction was started by adding a certain amount of water to this reactor. The volume of H2 obtained at certain time intervals by the water exchange method was continuously recorded in a scaled burette. In these hydrolysis tests, the effects of Co metal loading amount, NaBH4 concentration, NaOH concentration and temperature were examined. The performance of the catalyst was considered as the average HGR (ml/mingcat −1). The synthesis steps and H2 gas production scheme of the Co3O4-supported SCQD catalyst are given in Fig. 1 . Reusability experiments for the Co3O4-supported SCQD catalyst were performed six consecutive times. At the end of the experiment, after removing a large amount of liquid remaining in the reactor, new experiments were carried out by adding new NaBH4.

Fig. 2 shows the optical properties of SCQD and Co3O4 doped SCQD samples by UV–Vis(a) and fluorescence spectroscopy(b). For SCQD, an absorbance peak is observed in UV–Vis at approximately 287 nm, which is assigned to the π → π* transition due to the sp2 fields of the CC for carbon atoms [42,43]. Similarly, an excitation wavelength maximum emission intensity of approximately 320 nm was observed in fluorescence spectroscopy for SCQD. However, significant changes in the optical behaviour of SCQD were observed in the presence of Co particles. In the presence of Co particles, there are decreases in both fluorescence and UV absorbance intensity of SCQD. Although the PL phenomena for CQDs cannot be fully explained due to their complex behaviour, the change in particle size of CDs and the distribution of different surface energy traps are considered to be the cause of these behaviours. The functional groups formed by Co ions on the CQD surface can act as an emission trap for the π–π* transition of conjugated C=C. Therefore, the surface defects formed by metal ions and the particle size of CQDs are considered to be the factors causing the PL mechanism. At the same time, there is a quantum confinement effect in CQDs with lower particle sizes. The PL band gap increases with a decrease in the particle size of CQDs [44]. Moreover, this result may also support the successful attachment of Co particles to the SCQD surface.

The morphologies of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT (a, b, c, d) and SCQD-HT(g) nanocomposites with the TEM analyses are given in Fig. 3 . Some bright and dark areas on the supported catalyst surface can be attributed to the formation of porous structures and Co/Co3O4 particles. It can be seen that a large number of Co and Co3O4 nanoparticles are distributed as homogeny in the matrix of SCQD (Fig. 3a, b, c, d). The average particle size for the SCQD-HT nanocomposite was approximately 8.86 nm (Fig. 3h). However, the average particle size for the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite was approximately 3.27 nm (Fig. 3e). This average particle size value for the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite is quite small. Thus, by reducing the average particle size, the accumulation of Co or Co3O4 particles on the catalyst support was prevented. By reducing the agglomeration of Co or Co3O4 particles and the formation of smaller metal particles, the formation of a new catalytic system with higher surface area and high catalytic activity can be mentioned [45]. Thus, the particle size of SCQD-supported Co3O4 particles enables the reactants to reach the active catalyst surface more easily. Due to its large surface area/volume ratio, it may be more suitable considering the reaction mechanism of Holbrook and Twist [46,47]. Fig. 3e shows the EDS analysis spectrum of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite. The presence of C, O, N and Co atoms was confirmed by EDS analysis. Thus, it can be stated that Co or Co3O4 particles were successfully formed on the SCQD support. By EDS analysis, the contents of C, O, N and Co atoms of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite were found to be 6.53 %, 1.53 %, 16.03 % and 67.54 %, respectively.

XPS was used to analyse the chemical state of different elements in the structure of the Co3O4-CQD-HT nanocomposite (Fig. 4 a). XPS survey spectrum showed the presence of C, O, and Co atoms in the structure of Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite. However, a peak belonging to the N atom was not seen in the structure, probably due to the low content. However, N atom content was detected in EDS analyses. The spectrum of C1s in the structure of Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite (Fig. 4b) can be divided into four peaks: 284, 285.1, 286.02 and 286.95 eV. The peaks at 284, 285.1, 286.02 and 286.95 eV can be attributed to C–C, C=C, C–O/C–O–C, and O-C=O bonds, respectively [48–50]. The peaks of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) at 530.9, 532.3 and 533.1 eV can be attributed to the presence of oxides (Co-O), oxygen atoms attached to carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups, respectively [51,52]. The XPS spectrum for Co2p (Fig. 4d) can be divided into five peaks at 780.01, 782.32, 786.51, 789.99, 795.1, and 797.53 eV. In Co2p peak deconvolution, the peaks at binding energy values of 780.01 eV and 782.32 eV can be assigned to Co3+ 2p3/2 and Co2+ 2p3/2 components, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at binding energy values of 795.1 and 797.53 eV can also be attributed to the Co3+ 2p1/2 and Co2+ 2p1/2 peaks, respectively. These results confirm that both Co2+ and Co3+ species coexist in the catalyst structure [53]. Satellite peaks representing the presence of Co2+ species in the catalyst structure at 786.51 and 789.99 eV can be accepted [54]. The C, O and Co contents of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst were found to be 41.14 %, 36.94 %, and 21.91 % by weight, respectively. These results obtained by XPS are quite different from the values found by EDS analysis. As a third analysis, the cobalt content in the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst was found to be 39.5 % by weight by ICP-OES. Possibly, the reason for this result may be that only the of Co valence state, which are embedded in the carbon layer support material and difficult to find, is detected by XPS analysis. Additionally, strong interactions between C or O atoms of metallic Co nanoparticles on the Co3O4-SCQD-HT surface may also contribute to this result [55].

Fig. 5 a shows the XRD pattern of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst. In the XRD pattern, it can be seen that the carbonaceous material has an amorphous structure. For Co3O4-SCQD-HT, the peak located at approximately 45° corresponds to the standard spectrum of Co3O4 (400) (JCPDS, No. 01–074-1657). Clearly, this peak most likely corresponds to bulk Co3O4 for Co3O4-SCQD-HT. Additionally, this finding can be interpreted as a higher degree of graphitization in the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst structure. It has been reported that electronic conductivity is increased by transition metals that support the graphitization of carbonaceous materials [56]. The specific surface area of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst was carried out with nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis at 77 K. As shown in Fig. 5b, it indicates the type IV isotherm. The specific surface area of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst was found to be 24.07 m2/g. In addition, the average pore size of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst is 13.70 nm with the BJH pore size studies (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5d shows the FTIR spectrum of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite. The peaks appearing around 3300 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum can be attributed to C-OH/NH stretching vibrations. In the FT-IR spectrum, the peaks between 1500 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 correspond to Bronsted and Lewis acid regions, which are important for catalytic applications. These results showed that the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst has significant Lewis acid sites. In the FTIR spectrum for Co3O4-SCQD-HT, the peaks at 1138 cm−1, 1468 cm−1, and 1630 cm−1 are assigned to C-O and C-OH vibrations. The successful doping of Co metal ions on the SCQD support material can be confirmed by the band at 754 cm−1 [57,58]. For Co3O4-SCQD-HT, there is a significant increase in peak vibration intensities between 2100 and 2350 cm−1. The reason for this result can be attributed to the adsorption of a significant amount of CO2 [59,60]. In the literature, bands in the range of 2000–2300 cm−1 have also been attributed to multivalent inorganic hydride or oxide components [61].

The examination of temperature effect on catalyst preparation by hydrothermal process is given in Fig. 6 a. Temperature values of 120, 135, 150, 165 and 180 °C were preferred in the hydrothermal process. The activities of the catalysts obtained after loading Co metal on the SCQD samples at these temperature values were examined by H2 production using 0.2 g NaBH4 at 30 °C and 40 mg of catalyst in a 5 % NaOH environment. HGR values obtained with Co-SCQD catalysts obtained at temperature values of 120, 135, 150, 165 and 180 °C were found to be 5656, 6300, 6798, 4492, and 4406 mlmin−1gcat −1, respectively. There is an increase in the HGR value as a result of increasing the hydrothermal temperature from 120 °C to 150 °C. However, as a result of increasing the hydrothermal temperature from 150 °C to 180 °C, there is a significant decrease in the HGR value, probably due to the deterioration of the pore structure of the SCQD support material. The optimum hydrothermal temperature was determined as 150 °C. Additionally, activation time upon catalyst preparation by hydrothermal process was also examined (Fig. 6b). Values of 2, 4, 8 and 16 h were used for hydrothermal activation time. After Co metal loading on SCQD samples at a hydrothermal temperature of 150 °C and different hydrothermal activation times, the activities of the catalysts obtained were examined by H2 production using 0.2 g NaBH4 at 30C and 40 mg of catalyst in a 5 % NaOH environment. HGR values obtained with Co-SCQD catalysts at 150 °C hydrothermal temperature and 2, 4, 8, and 16 h’ hydrothermal activation times were found to be 5033, 6798, 4530, and 3939 ml/mingcat −1, respectively. It can be stated that hydrothermal activation time, like hydrothermal temperature, is also effective on catalyst production. The hydrolysis data obtained as a result of these periods are given in Fig. 6b. The best hydrothermal activation time was determined to be 4 h. At the same time, the effect of different Co metal concentrations on catalyst preparation by hydrothermal process was also examined. H2 data obtained from NaBH4 hydrolysis with the catalyst obtained with 2, 3, 5 and 7 mmol Co metal concentration ratios at 150 °C hydrothermal temperature and 4 h’ hydrothermal activation time are given in Fig. 6c. HGR values obtained from NaBH4 hydrolysis with 0.2 g NaBH4 at 30 °C and 40 mg catalyst in 5 % NaOH environment with 2, 3, 5 and 7 mmol Co metal concentration ratios were found to be 6798, 7461, 7289 and 5755 mlmin−1gcat −1, respectively. The highest HGR rate was obtained with the use of 3 mmol Co metal.

H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis of catalyst systems prepared under different conditions was examined (Fig. 7 a). For this purpose, Co (0), Co3O4-sucrose, Co3O4-SCQD and Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst systems were used. HGR values of 1928, 3856, 4960, and 6994 mlmin−1gcat −1 were found with Co (0), Co3O4-sucrose, Co3O4-SCQD and Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst systems, respectively. There were significant increases in HGR values with the use of only sucrose support and SCQD prepared with the hydrothermal process. The reason for these increases can be attributed to both the lack of agglomeration of Co3O4 species on the support and the small particle size of SCQD obtained by the hydrothermal process.

The effects of different solvent systems were examined to further increase H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis with Co3O4-SCQD-HT on a sucrose-based CQD-supported catalyst (Fig. 7b). In addition to water, methanol and ethanol solvents were also tested in the catalyst synthesis during the hydrothermal process stage. HGR values of 7041, 18,426 and 27,555 mlmin−1gcat −1 were obtained with Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalysts synthesized during the hydrothermal process with water, ethanol and methanol solvents, respectively. The HGR values of the catalysts obtained in ethanol and methanol medium provided an improvement of approximately 2.5 and 4 times, respectively, compared to the catalyst prepared in water medium. These rates are quite high. The reason for the high catalytic activity can be attributed to the hydrophilic/hydrophobic nature of the solvents and the fact that the alcoholic solvents used are not strongly acidic or oxidizing. Thus, Co metal particles prepared by hydrothermal process in alcoholic solvents provide a higher dispersion on the SCQD carbon support material [62]. Moreover, the catalytic performance of the catalyst prepared with alcoholic solvents may be significantly related to the change of activation energy of the intermediate complex or adjacent metal active sites by solvent adsorption on the catalyst [63]. Fig. 7c presents the effect of NaBH4 concentration on catalytic H2 production, 5 % NaOH and 1 %, 2 %, 4 % and 8 % NaBH4 concentrations by Co3O4-SCQD-HT at 30 °C. HGR values of 21333, 27555, 34,413 and 22,598 mlmin−1gcat −1 were found with 1 %, 2 %, 4 % and 8 % NaBH4 concentrations, respectively. Therefore, there is an increase in HGR values by increasing the amount of NaBH4 up to 4 %. In addition, there is a decrease in the HGR value as a result of increasing the amount of NaBH4 by more than 4 %. Additionally, the effect of NaOH concentration on the catalytic hydrolysis performance of NaBH4 by Co3O4-SCQD-HT was investigated at 0 %, 2.5 %, 5 %, 7.5 % and 10 % at 30 °C. (Fig. 7d). HGR values obtained from NaBH4 hydrolysis by Co3O4-SCQD-HT at 0 %, 2.5 %, 5 %, 7.5 % and 10 % NaOH were obtained as 19965, 24580, 27851, 25457, and 22,115 mlmin−1gcat −1, respectively. These results show that NaOH concentration affects NaBH4 hydrolysis.

The effect of temperature on the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by Co3O4-SCQD-HT in a 0.2 g NaBH4 solution containing 4 % NaOH is presented in Fig. 8 a. The HGR values obtained from the hydrolysis of NaBH4 with Co3O4-SCQD-HT for temperatures of 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C were 27555, 46667, 64,905 and 88,512 mlmin−1gcat −1, respectively. The H2 production rate was significantly affected by temperature. Arrhenius plot was used to calculate the activation energy of NaBH4 hydrolysis with Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst. From the slope of the graph, the activation energy for NaBH4 hydrolysis of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst was 29.92 kJ/mol. H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis of Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst.

The reusability performance of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst in 0.2 g NaBH4 solution containing 4 % NaOH is presented in Fig. 8b. After each experiment, samples were collected and dried again by centrifugation, and then fresh NaBH4 solution was added to the solution for new experiments. In the reusability performance of this hydrolysis reaction of the Co3O4-SCQD catalyst, which was carried out in six cycles, the reaction was completed in 2.75 min in the first cycle, while this reaction was completed in 3.25 min in the second cycle. The reaction completion time for the sixth cycle was 9 min. There is some decrease in catalytic activity with each cycle. However, 100 % H2 conversion was achieved in all cycles.

It was shown by TEM analyses that the particle sizes of SCQD composites containing Co3O4 were quite small. Besides, TEM images of SCQD composites containing Co3O4 also showed a homogeneous distribution of catalyst particles without any aggregation. Thus, the homogeneous distribution of Co3O4 particles on the SCQD support without any agglomeration improves catalytic performance by contributing to easier mass transfer of reactants to the catalyst surface. In addition, SCQD-supported Co3O4 particles prepared by hydrothermal method in methanol and ethanol alcohol solvents compared to water gives a very high catalytic activity. The surface tension of water is higher compared to ethanol and methanol alcohol solvents. It is possible that as the surface tension of water is high, the collection of Co particles becomes easier, the particle size increases and the active areas decrease. Using solvents with lower surface tension, such as methanol and ethanol, prevents the aggregation of Co nanoparticles. There are similar approaches in the literature [64]. In addition, water molecules, which have a higher dielectric constant and are more polar than alcohols, tend to easily form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups on the SCQD surface, preventing the dispersion of Co3O4 particles on the SCQD, resulting in the formation of larger particles [65]. Another important feature is that the higher the solvent viscosity, the lower the diffusion rate of the solution species, which may also cause agglomeration. Viscosity values for methanol and water are 0.793 and 1.793 mN·s/m2, respectively [24,66]. Therefore, more agglomeration of Co3O4 species on SCQD is expected in the aquatic environment.

In addition to particle size, surface active regions are one of the most important features for catalytic performance. A significant presence of oxidized species was also detected in the FTIR spectrum of Co3O4-SCQD-HT particles. The oxidized species in the catalyst acting as Lewis acid sites may facilitate OH absorption [67]. These results show that oxidized species should also be considered due to the high catalytic activity obtained for Co3O4-SCQD-HT. [68,69]. In the first stage, the formation of an active complex structure between NaBH4 and the surface of Co3O4-SCQD-HT particles occurs. BH4 − transfers the reactant H− to the catalyst active site. In addition, it contributes to the formation of an H2 molecule by giving H+ in the structure of the water molecule. As a result of these interactions, the remaining OH– and BH3 also react and form BH3(OH)− on the catalyst surface. This BH3(OH)− interacts with another water molecule, producing a new H2 molecule and BH2(OH)2 − ion. This process is repeated until B(OH)4 − ion is formed. The rapid occurrence of these reaction steps increases catalytic performance. Therefore, the presence of electron-rich or electron-poor regions on the catalyst system is essential for the adsorption of reactants in solution. XPS analyses showed the formation of Co3O4 in the catalytic system. Co3+ and Co2+ ions present in the SCQD-supported Co3O4 catalyst can serve as suitable sites for the adsorption of water and borohydride ions [15]. In addition, electron-withdrawing atoms in the SCQD structure can also contribute as Lewis bases to separate hydrogen ions from H2O. Thus, it makes a significant contribution to the formation of H2 from NaBH4 hydrolysis with the synergistic effect between Co3O4 and SCQD.

In this study, an effective catalyst was synthesized using Co3O4 metal nanoparticles dispersed on CQD support material from sucrose by hydrothermal method for H2 production from NaBH4 hydrolysis. Optimization of hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal activation times, Co metal loading and different solvent environment parameters were carried out for the synthesis of Co3O4 metal nanoparticles on CQD from sucrose. NaBH4 concentration, NaOH concentration and temperature parameters were optimized for H2 production experiments. HGR values of 7041, 18,426 and 27,555 mlmin−1gcat −1 were obtained with Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalysts synthesized during the hydrothermal process with water, ethanol and methanol solvents, respectively. TEM, EDS, nitrogen adsorption/desorption, XRD, FTIR, ICP-OES and XPS analyses were used for the characterization study. The average particle size of the Co3O4-SCQD-HT nanocomposite was approximately 3.27 nm. The presence of C, O, N and Co atoms was confirmed by EDS and XPS analysis. For Co3O4-SCQD-HT, the peak located at approximately 45° corresponds to the standard spectrum of Co3O4 (400). The peaks in the ranges of 1500 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 for Co3O4-SCQD-HT in FT-IR spectrum were the Bronsted and Lewis acid regions. These results showed that the Co3O4-SCQD-HT catalyst has significant Lewis acid sites. XPS analyses show the formation of Co3O4 in the catalytic system. Co3+ and Co2+ ions present in the SCQD-supported Co3O4 catalyst.

Erhan Onat: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mehmet Sait Izgi: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ömer Şahin: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Cafer Saka: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
